analogy: Think of "Social Change and Social Order in Rural and Urban Society" through the chapter's own opening images: 21 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY CHAPTER 2 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY It is often said that change is the only unchanging aspect of society. Anyone living in modern society does not need to... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Social Change and Social Order in Rural and Urban Society is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 21 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY CHAPTER 2 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY It is often said that change is the only unchanging aspect of society.

The text explains that anyone living in modern society does not need to be reminded that constant change is among the most permanent features of our society.

The text explains that in fact, the discipline of sociology itself emerged as an effort to make sense of the rapid changes that Wester n European society had experienced between the seventeenth and nineteenth centuries.

The text explains that but though social change seems such a common and obvious fact about moder n life, it is – comparatively speaking – a very new and recent fact.

The text explains that it is estimated that human beings have existed on planet earth for approximately 500,000 (five lakh) years, but they have had a civilised existence for only about 6,000 years.

The text explains that of these civilised years, it is only in the last 400 years that we have seen constant and rapid change; even within these years of change, the pace has accelerated only in the last 100 years.

The text explains that because the speed with which change happens has been increasing steadily, it is probably true that in the last hundred years, change has been faster in the last fifty years than in the first fifty.

The text explains that and within the last fifty years, the world may have changed more in the last twenty years than in the first thirty… The Clock of Human History Human beings have existed on earth for about half a million years.

The text explains that agriculture, the necessary basis of fixed settlements, is only about twelve thousand years old.

The text explains that civilisations date back no more than six thousand years or so.

The text explains that if we were to think of the entire span of human existence thus far as a day (stretching from midnight to midnight), agriculture would have come into existence at 11:56 pm and civilisations at 11:57.

The text explains that the development of modern societies would get underway only at 11:59 and 30 seconds!

The text explains that yet perhaps as much change has taken place in the last thirty seconds of this human day as in all the time leading up to it.

The text explains that from: Anthony Giddens,2004 Sociology, 4th edition, p.40.

The text explains that 22 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY Activity 1 Talk to your elders and make a list of the things in your life that: (a) did not exist when your parents were your age; and (b) did not exist when your grandparents were your age.

The text explains that eg: Black & white/colour TV; milk in plastic bags, zip fasteners on clothes; plastic buckets; etc. – did it exist in your parents’/ grandparents’ childhood?

The text explains that attempts to further qualify it usually try to classify it by its sources or causes; by its nature, or the kind of impact it has on society; and by its pace or speed.

The text explains that the value of the coin was directly linked to the value of the gold or silver it contained.

The text explains that but by far the most common way of classifying social change is by its causes or sources.

The text explains that there are five broad types of sources o r c a u s e s o f s o c i a l c h a n g e : e n v i r o n m e n t a l , t e c h n o l o g i c a l , economic, political and cultural.

The text explains that but the extent to which the environment influences society has been decreasing over time with the increase in technological resources.

The text explains that so it is perhaps more accurate to say that the effect of Students in a Vocational Training Lab Source: https//www.msde.gov.in/sites/default/files/2019-09/National%20Skill%20Development %20Mission.pdf

The text explains that sudden and catastrophic events such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, floods, or tidal waves (like the tsunami that hit Indonesia, Sri Lanka, the Andaman Islands and parts of Tamil Nadu in December 2004) can change societies quite drastically.

The text explains that the discovery of steam power allowed emerging forms of large scale industry to use of a source of energy that was not only far stronger than animals or human beings, but was also capable of continuous operation without the need for rest.

The text explains that sometimes changes in economic organisation that are not directly technological can also change society.

The text explains that 30 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY cannot be denied that universal adult franchise serves as a powerful norm that exerts pressure on every society and every government.

The text explains that 31 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY of advertising expenditure are now directed at women, and this in turn has effects on the media.

The text explains that 33 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY strata are differently positioned with respect to command over economic resources, social status and political power.

The text explains that thus, technically, every act of violence is seen as being directed against the state.

The text explains that in economic and administrative terms, the distinction between rural and urban settlements is usually made on the basis of two major factors: population density and the proportion of agriculture related economic activities.

The text explains that (Contrary to appearances, size is not always decisive; it becomes difficult to separate large villages and small towns on the basis of population size alone.) Thus, cities and towns have a much higher density of population — or the number of persons per unit area, such as a square km — than villages.

The text explains that given the directions in which modern societies have developed, the process of urbanisation has been experienced in most countries.

The text explains that moreover, the relative power of the dominant sections is much more because they control most avenues of employment, and most resources of all kinds.

The text explains that so the poor have to depend on the dominant sections since there are no alternative sources of employment or support.

The text explains that even if they don’t directly affect labour demand, technological or economic changes can change the economic power of different groups and thus set in motion a chain of changes.

The text explains that high population density places a great premium on space and creates very complex problems of logistics.

The text explains that the urban transport system is directly and severely affected by the location of residential areas relative to industrial and commercial workplaces.

The text explains that commuting becomes a way of life and an ever present source of possible disruption.

The text explains that the transport system has a direct impact on the ‘quality of life’ of working people in the city.

The text explains that across the world, the city centre – or the core area of the original city – has had many changes of fortune.

The text explains that explain with examples other than those in the text.

The text explains that describe some kinds of environment-related social change.

The text explains that what are some kinds of changes brought about by technology and the economy?

The text explains that what is meant by social order and how is it maintained?

The text explains that what is authority and how is it related to domination and the law?

The text explains that how are a village, town and city distinguished from each other?

The text explains that what are some features of social order in rural areas?

The text explains that what are some of the challenges to social order in urban areas?

The text explains that 49 SOCIAL CHANGE AND SOCIAL ORDER IN RURAL AND URBAN SOCIETY REFERENCES GIDDENS, Antony.

The text explains that urban Sociology, Readings in Sociology and Social Anthropology series).

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 2: Social Change and Social Order in Rural and Urban Society
